region_of_origin: India — coastal states of Kerala, Maharashtra, and West Bengal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no prawn-specific biryani subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani — rice-based mixed dish with meat/seafood
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers biryani as a rice-based mixed dish; no prawn-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for prawn biryani or biryani dish found in publicly available SNOMED CT browser as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Prawn Biryani weighs approximately 450 g and provides around 750 kcal, making it a substantial single-meal serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR — National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides composition data for prawn curry and cooked basmati rice separately. Values for Prawn Biryani are adapted by combining prawn (approximately 30% by weight) and cooked basmati rice (approximately 55% by weight) with ghee/oil and spice components (approximately 15% by weight), consistent with standard recipe proportions. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked white rice. All values rounded to two decimal places.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Prawn Biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 is estimated using basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~50–58 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). The high protein content from prawns (~7.8 g/100 g) and fat from ghee further attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a low-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 85.0) / 100 = 42.5 g (based on 450 g serving containing ~18.89 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 85.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 244 mg per 100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,100 mg sodium, representing nearly 48% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by WHO.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Prawn Biryani to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding extra salt or salty condiments, and pair with low-sodium side dishes such as plain raita made without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Basmati rice provides a moderate carbohydrate load (~85 g per 450 g serving); however, the low GI (~50) of basmati rice combined with the protein from prawns and fat from ghee attenuates the postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Prawn Biryani in controlled portions (200–250 g), prioritising the prawn component over rice, monitoring blood glucose response, and avoiding high-sugar accompaniments such as sweetened raita or sugary drinks.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.67 g per 100 g (12.0 g per 450 g serving) from ghee and cholesterol 55.56 mg per 100 g (250 mg per 450 g serving) from prawns are relevant for cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request preparation with minimal ghee or substitute with a heart-healthy oil, and note that prawns provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids which partially offset the saturated fat concern.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Prawns are a major crustacean shellfish allergen.
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Individuals with a known shellfish or crustacean allergy must avoid Prawn Biryani entirely; cross-contamination risk is high in shared kitchen environments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Biryani is a sophisticated coastal Indian rice dish that combines succulent fresh prawns with aromatic long-grain basmati rice, prepared using the traditional dum (slow steam) cooking method. The prawns are first marinated in yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, red chili powder, and garam masala, then briefly sautéed to preserve their juicy texture. Parboiled basmati rice is layered alternately with the spiced prawns, crispy fried onions, fresh mint, and coriander, then sealed and slow-cooked over low heat so that steam infuses the rice with the delicate seafood essence. The dish is particularly popular in the coastal states of Kerala (where it is known as Chemmeen Biryani), Maharashtra (Kolambi Biryani), and West Bengal. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.7 g fat, and 18.9 g carbohydrate. Prawns contribute high-quality complete protein, vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg/100 g), selenium (18 mcg/100 g), and niacin (3.2 mg/100 g). The dish has a low glycaemic index (~50) due to basmati rice and the protein-fat matrix slowing carbohydrate absorption. Sodium content is moderately high at 244 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The dish is halal-suitable and gluten-free in traditional preparation. It is best served as a festive main course at lunch or dinner, accompanied by cooling raita and a wedge of lemon.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani high in sodium?
How much protein in prawn biryani?
What is the difference between prawn biryani and chicken biryani?
Can I eat prawn biryani if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using standard recipe proportions for prawn biryani (prawn ~30%, basmati rice ~55%, ghee/spices ~15%). No single approved-source entry for prawn biryani as a composite dish exists; values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked shrimp and cooked white rice. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from University of Sydney / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for prawn biryani as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. water_g is set to -1 for the same reason. 2. trans_fat_g (0.11 g/100 g) is a low but non-zero value derived from ghee content in the recipe; ghee contains trace naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid). 3. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values in the input were sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2); these have been replaced with values adapted from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. Portions in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (350g)'; the canonical serving size has been set to 450 g (1 plate) consistent with standard restaurant portions. A secondary reference serving of 350 g is noted. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for prawn biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for prawn biryani exists as of 2026. 7. This is an Indian coastal dish, not a Singaporean dish; Chinese, Malay mandatory language fields are not required under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for Indian cuisine.
